title: The next era of AI coding
summary: Michael Truell, CEO of Cursor, presents data showing rapid adoption of AI coding agents in 2025, with 30% of Cursor's pull requests being fully agent-developed end-to-end and enterprise code going from 15% to 75% AI-generated in a year. He argues that the role of software engineers is shifting toward managing teams of autonomous agents rather than writing code directly.
writeup: A Cursor co-founder traces the shift in software development across three eras: manual text editing, AI tab-completion and single-agent coding, and an emerging teams era of many parallel autonomous agents. The talk anchors the transition in hard internal data, including a 15x year-over-year rise in agent requests on Cursor and enterprise customers moving from roughly 15% to 75% AI-generated code inside a year. The through-line is that engineering roles are reconstituting around delegation, review, and managing fleets of agents rather than writing syntax directly.

similar.title: How are coding agents changing software engineering?
How Cursor builds agentic workflows across the SDLC
How Intuit, DoorDash, and Atlassian are adopting AI coding
What Is Agentic Coding? How AI Agents Modernize Code
Are senior developers going back to writing code by hand?
What happens when agents get their own computers

key_points: Cursor's internal usage data shows agent requests grew more than 15x year over year, and the ratio flipped from 10x more tab-accepts than agent requests at the start of 2025 to more agent requests than tab-accepts by mid-year, with each agent request representing a much larger body of work than a tab completion.
30% of pull requests at Cursor itself are now developed end-to-end by an agent with no human intervention, running autonomously in a cloud environment for hours or even days at a time.
In Cursor's enterprise segment, the share of AI-generated code rose from roughly 15-20% about a year ago to approximately 75% today, with engineers delegating syntax entirely to agents.

As organizations move into the teams era, engineers increasingly function as agent managers: delegating tasks, reviewing outputs, and context-switching across dozens of parallel agent colleagues rather than editing code directly.

Cursor ran an experimental project in which an agent team, with no humans in the loop, autonomously built a mostly functional web browser prototype over one week, producing 3 million lines of code across many concurrent agents and iterating through visible rendering improvements day by day.

The risk of fast autonomous code generation is unsustainable output riddled with bad architecture decisions and bugs, so the teams era demands proportionally more time spent in review and testing rather than less.
